perturbation.original_text: 1.4 "Applicable Laws" means the applicable provisions of any and all national, supranational, regional, state and local laws, treaties, statutes, rules, regulations, administrative codes, industry codes, guidance, ordinances, judgments, decrees, directives, injunctions, orders or permits (including Regulatory Approvals) of or from any court, arbitrator, Regulatory Authority or governmental agency or authority having jurisdiction over or related to the subject item, including the FCPA, export control, embargo, trade sanction and data privacy laws, regulations, rules and licenses, and other laws and regulations pertaining to domestic or international corruption, commercial bribery, fraud, embezzlement or money laundering.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 "Applicable Laws" means the provisions of national laws, regulations, and rules of any court, arbitrator, Regulatory Authority or governmental agency or authority having jurisdiction over or related to the subject item, including the FCPA, export control, embargo, trade sanction and data privacy laws, regulations, rules and licenses, and other laws and regulations pertaining to domestic or international corruption, commercial bribery, fraud, embezzlement or money laundering.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Applicable Laws" was narrowed to only include *national laws*. This contradicts established legal principles that recognize the binding authority of international treaties and agreements under certain conditions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Supremacy Clause, Article VI, United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: By limiting 'Applicable Laws' to only 'national laws', the contract contradicts the Supremacy Clause of the U.S. Constitution, which establishes that treaties made under the authority of the United States are the supreme law of the land, superseding conflicting state laws. This limitation could lead to disputes over the enforceability of treaty obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article VI | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article VI Primary tabs All debts contracted and engagements entered into, before the adoption of this Constitution, shall be as valid against the United States under this Constitution, as under the Confederation. This Constitution, and the laws of the United States which shall be made in pursuance thereof; and all treaties made, or which shall be made, under the authority of the United States, shall be the supreme law of the land; and the judges in every state shall be bound thereby, anything in the Constitution or laws of any State to the contrary notwithstanding. The Senators and Representatives before mentioned, and the members of the several state legislatures, and all executive and judicial officers, both of the United States and of the several states, shall be bound by oath or affirmation, to support this Constitution; but no religious test shall ever be required as a qualification to any office or public trust under the United States. ‹ Article V up Article VII › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the definition of 'Applicable Laws' to 'national laws,' excluding supranational, regional, state, and local laws, treaties, etc., as seen in the original text. This directly contradicts the Supremacy Clause, as articulated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which states that treaties made under the authority of the United States 'shall be the supreme law of the land.' By narrowing the definition of applicable laws, the modified contract undermines the enforceability of treaty obligations, which are considered supreme under the U.S. Constitution, thus creating a legal contradiction as the contract now conflicts with established legal principles.

perturbation.original_text: 1.33 "Indirect Losses" means any damages or other losses involving, but only to the extent of, any loss of profits, diminution in value, or incidental, indirect, consequential, special or punitive damages.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.33 "Indirect Losses" means any damages or other losses involving any loss of profits or incidental damages.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting 'Indirect Losses' to 'loss of profits or incidental damages', the changed text removes 'diminution in value, consequential, special or punitive damages'. This can create ambiguity in cases of breach of contract or product liability where those removed items can be very relevant damages. These terms have a clear legal definition. The change will be against the legal intent of the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 2 of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification of the definition of "Indirect Losses" undermines the UCC’s provision for consequential damages in breach of contract cases. UCC § 2-715 allows for the recovery of consequential damages, including losses resulting from general or particular requirements and needs of which the seller at the time of contracting had reason to know and which could not reasonably be prevented by cover or otherwise. By not including consequential damages in the definition of Indirect Losses, the party could argue that they are not responsible for damages they should have been.

perturbation.justification: The modification of the definition of "Indirect Losses" in the changed text creates a legal contradiction under the category of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction. By removing 'diminution in value, consequential, special or punitive damages,' the changed text deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable. The Law Explanation states that UCC § 2-715 allows for the recovery of consequential damages, including losses resulting from general or particular requirements and needs of which the seller at the time of contracting had reason to know and which could not reasonably be prevented by cover or otherwise. The altered definition conflicts with the UCC’s provision for consequential damages in breach of contract cases, creating a contradiction. Though snippet 1 is missing, and snippet 2 is just an informational excerpt about Uniform Law Commission, it does reference the UCC. The UCC, as cited by the Law Explanation, is the critical support.

perturbation.original_text: 8.1 Confidential Information. Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing by the Parties, the Parties agree that during the Term and for [***] thereafter, the receiving Party (the "Receiving Party") shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose or use for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement or the Supply Agreement any Information or materials furnished to it or its Affiliates by or on behalf of the other Party (the "Disclosing Party") or its Affiliates pursuant to this Agreement, the Supply Agreement, the Confidentiality Agreement or any other written agreement between the Parties or their Affiliates, in any form (written, oral, photographic, electronic, magnetic, or otherwise), including all information concerning the Product and any other technical or business information of whatever nature (collectively, "Confidential Information" of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.changed_text: 8.1 Confidential Information. Except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing by the Parties, the Parties agree that during the Term and for [***] thereafter, the receiving Party (the "Receiving Party") shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose or use for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement or the Supply Agreement any Information or materials furnished to it or its Affiliates by or on behalf of the other Party (the "Disclosing Party") or its Affiliates pursuant to this Agreement, the Supply Agreement, the Confidentiality Agreement or any other written agreement between the Parties or their Affiliates, in any form (written, oral, photographic, electronic, magnetic, or otherwise), including all information concerning the Product but excludes any other technical or business information of whatever nature (collectively, "Confidential Information" of the Disclosing Party).
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Confidential Information" was narrowed to include only all information concerning the Product. This contradicts intellectual property protection, because it is much wider than only all information concerning the Product. Technical or business information should be protected.
perturbation.law_explanation: By specifically excluding 'any other technical or business information of whatever nature' from the definition of Confidential Information, the contract fails to protect trade secrets as defined by the Trade Secrets Act. The Act defines a trade secret broadly to include various forms of information that a business uses to gain an advantage over its competitors. This exclusion weakens the protection of valuable company assets.
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to exclude 'any other technical or business information of whatever nature,' which contradicts the intent to protect trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that this exclusion weakens the protection of valuable company assets. Although the scraped law snippet focuses on the Economic Espionage Act, its listing of sections related to trade secrets and economic espionage suggests that broad protection of business information is legally recognized, supporting the argument that limiting 'Confidential Information' only to 'information concerning the Product' creates a legal contradiction.
